_meta.description: Assessment strategies and scaffolding techniques for multilingual learners

strategies.core_principle: Gather evidence of learning to adjust instruction and provide actionable feedback in real time.
applicable_contexts: Assess content understanding and language development
implementation_steps: Co-define goals/success criteria; collect varied evidence.
Give timely feedback; allow L1 to assess content knowledge accurately.

definitions.short_en: Ongoing assessment practices that gather evidence of learning to adjust instruction and provide feedback for multilingual learners.
definitions.long_en: Formative Assessment for Multilingual Learners involves systematic collection of evidence about student learning during instruction to make real-time adjustments and provide actionable feedback. For multilingual learners, this includes separating content knowledge from language proficiency, using multiple modalities for assessment, and allowing L1 use when appropriate to accurately gauge understanding. Teachers use various techniques such as exit tickets, thumbs up/down, peer discussions, and quick writes to monitor both content comprehension and language development. The approach emphasizes co-constructed success criteria, timely feedback, and student self-assessment opportunities. Effective formative assessment for multilingual learners recognizes that language development takes time and provides multiple ways for students to demonstrate their knowledge while building English proficiency.
look_fors: Multiple assessment modalities accommodating language proficiency levels
Separation of content knowledge from English language proficiency
Real-time instructional adjustments based on assessment evidence
Student self-assessment and goal-setting opportunities
non_examples: Only paper-and-pencil assessments requiring high English proficiency
Confusing content knowledge with English language ability

strategies.core_principle: Collect student work over time to document growth and provide authentic assessment opportunities.
implementation_steps: Establish clear portfolio criteria and organization system.
Include student reflections and goal-setting in both L1 and English.
Use portfolios for student-led conferences and growth celebrations.

definitions.short_en: Systematic collection of student work over time to document learning growth and provide authentic assessment.
definitions.long_en: Portfolio Assessment involves systematic collection and organization of student work over time to document learning growth, provide authentic assessment opportunities, and promote student reflection. For multilingual learners, portfolios can include work in both L1 and English, showing language development progression and content learning across languages. Students actively participate in selecting work, writing reflections, and setting goals for future learning. Portfolios may include writing samples, audio recordings, project documentation, self-assessments, and peer feedback. This approach allows teachers, students, and families to see growth over time rather than focusing on single-point assessments. Effective portfolio implementation includes clear criteria, regular conferences, and opportunities for students to share their learning journey with authentic audiences.
look_fors: Systematic collection and organization of work over time
Student participation in work selection and reflection processes
Documentation of growth in both content and language development
Regular portfolio conferences and goal-setting sessions
non_examples: Random collection of work without clear purpose or criteria
Teacher-only selection without student input or reflection
Portfolios that sit unused without regular review or conferences

strategies.core_principle: Engage students in providing structured feedback to peers using both L1 and English as appropriate.
implementation_steps: Teach specific feedback protocols and sentence frames.
Allow peer discussions in L1 before providing English feedback.
primary_benefits: Develops critical thinking and communication skills

definitions.short_en: Structured process where students provide feedback to peers using appropriate language supports and protocols.
definitions.long_en: Peer Assessment in Bilingual Settings involves teaching students to provide structured, constructive feedback to their peers using both L1 and English as appropriate for comprehension and communication. Students learn specific feedback protocols, use sentence frames for academic language practice, and engage in peer discussions that may include L1 processing before providing English feedback. This approach develops critical thinking, communication skills, and metacognitive awareness while providing multiple perspectives on learning. Teachers model respectful feedback practices and provide scaffolds for both giving and receiving feedback. The strategy is particularly effective in writing workshops, project presentations, and collaborative learning activities where peer input enhances the learning process.
look_fors: Structured feedback protocols and sentence frames for academic language
L1 discussion opportunities before English feedback provision
Respectful, constructive feedback practices modeled and practiced
Student development of critical thinking and communication skills
non_examples: Unstructured peer feedback without clear protocols or language support
Feedback that focuses on surface-level corrections without deeper learning
Peer assessment without teaching students how to give constructive feedback

strategies.core_principle: Provide multiple ways for students to demonstrate knowledge that don't rely heavily on English proficiency.
implementation_steps: Design assessments using visuals, demonstrations, and hands-on activities.
Allow choice in assessment format and language use when appropriate.
Focus on content knowledge rather than English language production.
primary_benefits: Provides equitable access to demonstrate learning
Reduces language barriers in content assessment

definitions.short_en: Assessment approaches that provide multiple ways for students to demonstrate knowledge without heavy reliance on English proficiency.
definitions.long_en: Alternative Assessment Methods involve designing assessment approaches that provide multiple ways for multilingual learners to demonstrate their knowledge and skills without heavy reliance on English language proficiency. These methods include performance-based assessments, visual demonstrations, hands-on projects, oral presentations with visual supports, and technology-enhanced assessments. Students may be given choices in assessment format and appropriate language use to show content mastery. The focus shifts from English language production to authentic demonstration of learning objectives. Alternative assessments often mirror real-world applications and provide more equitable access for students at various English proficiency levels. Implementation requires careful alignment with learning objectives while removing unnecessary language barriers that might mask content knowledge.
look_fors: Multiple assessment formats accommodating different learning styles and language levels
Focus on content knowledge demonstration rather than English production
Visual, hands-on, and performance-based assessment options
Student choice in assessment format when appropriate
non_examples: Only traditional paper-and-pencil tests requiring high English proficiency
Assessment methods that confuse language ability with content knowledge
One-size-fits-all assessments without consideration of language barriers

strategies.core_principle: Systematically assess students' development of academic language functions and structures.
implementation_steps: Identify specific language functions and structures for assessment.
Use rubrics that separate content knowledge from language proficiency.
Provide feedback on both content mastery and language development.

definitions.short_en: Systematic assessment of students' development in academic language functions and structures aligned with content learning.
definitions.long_en: Language Objectives Assessment involves systematically evaluating students' development of specific academic language functions, structures, and vocabulary within content area learning. Teachers identify target language objectives (such as comparing, explaining, arguing) and assess student progress using rubrics that separate content knowledge from language proficiency development. This approach aligns with frameworks like WIDA standards and SIOP methodology, providing specific feedback on both content mastery and language growth. Assessment methods include structured observations, language-focused rubrics, and documentation of student language use in academic contexts. The goal is to track academic language development over time while informing targeted language instruction that supports content learning objectives.
look_fors: Clear identification of specific language functions and structures for assessment
Rubrics that separate content knowledge from language proficiency
Systematic tracking of academic language development over time
Targeted feedback on both content and language objectives
non_examples: Assessment that only focuses on content without attention to language development
Vague language objectives without specific assessment criteria
Confusing content mastery with English language proficiency levels
